verdicts.title: exec() in command.lib.php - Shell Command Execution (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: No viable attack vector. Line 71: 'command -v %s 1>/dev/null' uses escapeshellarg($cmd) where $cmd is always a hardcoded command name ('diff') — never user-controlled. Lines 93-98: 'diff -u -- %s %s 2>/dev/null' uses escapeshellarg($a) and escapeshellarg($b) where $a and $b are created via tempnam() (system-generated temp file paths). The caller diffHTML() accepts $filepath from user POST but validates it with array_key_exists($filepath, $checksums) against official WordPress checksums from wordpress.org or GitHub API — only known WP core file paths are accepted. The escapeshellarg() calls further prevent any shell injection even if the whitelist were bypassed.
verdicts.evidence: Line 70-71: $command = sprintf('command -v %s 1>/dev/null', escapeshellarg($cmd)); @exec($command, $out, $err); Lines 93-98: @exec(sprintf('diff -u -- %s %s 2> /dev/null', escapeshellarg($a), escapeshellarg($b)), $out, $err); In diffHTML() line 131-132: if (!array_key_exists($filepath, $checksums)) { return error('Not an official WordPress file.'); }
verdicts.remediation: No remediation needed. Both exec() calls are protected with escapeshellarg() and the filepath is validated against an official whitelist before reaching the shell.

verdicts.title: Authentication and CSRF Protection on All Admin AJAX Actions
verdicts.attack_vector: All AJAX handlers are registered under wp_ajax_ only (not wp_ajax_nopriv_), requiring authenticated sessions. The sucuriscan_ajax() dispatcher calls checkPageVisibility() (current_user_can('manage_options')) and checkNonce() (wp_verify_nonce()) before dispatching. Individual handlers also independently check canManagePlugin(). The 2FA profile AJAX handlers verify per-user nonces in resolve_ajax_target_user(). No nopriv handlers exist in the codebase.
verdicts.evidence: function sucuriscan_ajax() { SucuriScanInterface::checkPageVisibility(); if (SucuriScanInterface::checkNonce()) { ... } }; canManagePlugin(): return current_user_can('manage_options'); checkNonce(): if (!wp_verify_nonce($nonce_value, $nonce_name)) { return false; }
verdicts.remediation: No remediation needed. Authentication and CSRF protection are properly layered throughout all admin actions.

verdicts.title: SHA1 Used for Git-Style File Checksums (False Positive for Password Usage)
verdicts.attack_vector: The sha1() call at api.lib.php line 1092 computes sha1('blob\x20' . strlen($content) . '\x00' . $content), which is the Git blob object hashing format defined in Git internals. This matches how GitHub API provides checksums for repository files. Used exclusively for file integrity comparison against external checksums — not for passwords, authentication tokens, or session secrets.
verdicts.evidence: if ($algorithm === 'sha1') { $content = SucuriScanFileInfo::fileContent($filename); return @sha1('blob\x20' . strlen($content) . '\x00' . $content); } // Git blob format, matches GitHub API checksums
verdicts.remediation: No remediation needed. This is correct Git-format blob hashing required to match GitHub's checksum API.

verdicts.title: MD5 Used for WordPress Core File Integrity Checking (Informational)
verdicts.attack_vector: MD5 via md5_file() is used to compute checksums of WordPress core files for comparison against checksums from the official WordPress.org API (which provides MD5 hashes). MD5 collision weaknesses are irrelevant here since the plugin compares against known-trusted checksums fetched over HTTPS — this is an integrity verification scenario, not a cryptographic authentication scenario. Preimage resistance (sufficient here) is not broken for MD5.
verdicts.remediation: Informational only. WordPress.org API uses MD5 checksums by design. No exploitable security risk in this context.

verdicts.title: HMAC-SHA1 in TOTP Implementation (Correct RFC 6238 Behavior)
verdicts.attack_vector: HMAC-SHA1 is mandated by RFC 4226 (HOTP) and RFC 6238 (TOTP), which the plugin implements for two-factor authentication. All major authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy, Microsoft Authenticator) use HMAC-SHA1 as the default. Using SHA-256 or SHA-512 variants would break interoperability with these apps. Not a security weakness.
verdicts.evidence: const DEFAULT_CRYPTO = 'sha1'; — used as algorithm parameter in hash_hmac() for TOTP code generation per RFC 6238
verdicts.remediation: Informational only. HMAC-SHA1 is the correct and required algorithm for RFC 6238 TOTP interoperability.

verdicts.title: XSS in Theme Toggle AJAX Handler (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: The sucuriscan_theme_toggle() function reads the current theme from user meta (a stored 'dark' or 'light' string) and toggles it to the opposite value. The variable $newTheme can only ever be the string 'dark' or 'light' (constrained by the ternary logic at line 136). Output at line 146 additionally uses esc_html(). The tainted path from stored user meta to output is fully constrained by the business logic before any escaping is applied.
verdicts.remediation: No remediation needed. Value is constrained to exactly two known-safe strings before esc_html() is applied.

verdicts.title: openssl_random_pseudo_bytes Without $crypto_strong Verification in IV Generation
verdicts.attack_vector: In option.lib.php getSecretRandomBytes() (line 1153-1155), openssl_random_pseudo_bytes() is called as a PHP 5 fallback for IV generation without checking the $crypto_strong output parameter. The generated IV is used in AES-256-GCM encryption of the WAF API key stored in wp_options. If the OS entropy pool were depleted (extremely rare), the IV could be predictable, potentially allowing an attacker with database read access to decrypt the stored WAF API key. Real-world risk is negligible: PHP 7+ systems (the vast majority) use random_bytes() first; openssl_random_pseudo_bytes() is in practice always cryptographically strong on modern Linux systems.
verdicts.evidence: private static function getSecretRandomBytes($length) { if (function_exists('random_bytes')) { return random_bytes($length); } if (function_exists('openssl_random_pseudo_bytes')) { return openssl_random_pseudo_bytes($length); /* missing $crypto_strong check */ } return false; }
verdicts.remediation: Add $crypto_strong check: $bytes = openssl_random_pseudo_bytes($length, $crypto_strong); if (!$crypto_strong) { return false; } return $bytes; This ensures the function returns false (and encryption is skipped) rather than using potentially weak bytes.

verdicts.title: Unsanitized Plugin/Theme Slug and Version Concatenated into External API URL Path
verdicts.attack_vector: In vulnerability.lib.php, vulnerabilitiesPluginAjax() and vulnerabilitiesThemeAjax() read 'slug' and 'version' from POST with no pattern constraint (SucuriScanRequest::post('slug') uses '.*' regex, returning esc_attr()-encoded value). These are concatenated directly into the URL: 'https://wp-plugin.sucuri.net/v1/vulnerabilities/' . $endpoint . '/' . $slug . '/' . $version without urlencode() or path-specific sanitization. An admin could supply slug='woocommerce/../../../admin' to traverse within Sucuri's API path space, or slug='woocommerce?key=val&' to inject query parameters. The WAF API Bearer key is sent in the Authorization header to whatever URL is constructed. Since the domain is hardcoded to wp-plugin.sucuri.net, true SSRF to internal hosts is not possible. Exploitation is limited to authenticated admins with manage_options and a valid nonce.
verdicts.evidence: $slug = SucuriscanRequest::post('slug'); $version = SucuriscanRequest::post('version'); // No path sanitization beyond esc_attr(). Then: $url = 'https://wp-plugin.sucuri.net/v1/vulnerabilities/' . $endpoint . '/' . $slug . '/' . $version; $args = array('headers' => array('Authorization' => 'Bearer ' . $key)); $response = wp_remote_get($url, $args);
verdicts.remediation: Sanitize slug and version before URL path construction: use sanitize_key() for slug (restricts to lowercase alphanumeric and hyphens) and a strict version pattern (e.g., '^[0-9.]+$') for version, then urlencode() both. Example: $url = 'https://wp-plugin.sucuri.net/v1/vulnerabilities/' . $endpoint . '/' . urlencode(sanitize_key($slug)) . '/' . urlencode(preg_replace('/[^0-9.]/', '', $version));
